schools.summary: Ellicott charts Saul's rising fury from spear-throwing insanity to open command; the literal Hebrew and LXX read 'he intended to kill David.'
Gill: Saul spoke to Jonathan first, hoping the heir would abandon a rival to his own succession.
Henry: never was enemy so cruel nor friend so kind; undisguised malice is least dangerous, and Providence made the trusted heir David's shield.
Clarke: only God's especial interposition could save David when every officer had orders to kill him.

themes: seed of the serpent and the woman

schools.summary: Gill: Jonathan's love of complacency for David was itself a providence, giving David warning of Saul's designs.
Ellicott: a king's slightest wish stirs unscrupulous men to murder; K&D reads the warning and plan as the modesty of a devoted son.
Clarke: the order was likely to slay David the next day, so Jonathan bids him be specially watchful then and hide.
Gill: Jonathan chose the hour of Saul's habitual morning walk to catch him calm and reasonable.
Ellicott: 'the field' was a garden or quiet place where the king resorted with his counsellors.
Henry: Jonathan waited for morning and managed his plea with the meekness of wisdom, risking his father's displeasure.
Gill: Jonathan set out David's goodness, wisdom, valour, and faithful service to king and country.
Ellicott and K&D: the heir most threatened by David pleads his rival's innocence and services, warning Saul not to sin against him.
Clarke: Jonathan was sure of David's innocence; Wesley: he could not speak good without hazard, doing the duty of a true friend and valiant man.
Gill: David put his life in his hand when none else in Israel's camp would face the Philistine.
K&D: the plea binds David's service and innocence together — why sin against innocent blood without cause?
Clarke: the pleadings are short but exceedingly cogent, an argument that could not be resisted.
Henry: David had in effect saved Saul's life and kingdom, so to slay him would be ingratitude, affront to God, and injury to Israel.
Gill: the LORD inclined Saul's mind so that Jonathan's words really affected and convicted him for the moment.
Ellicott: for a brief space the old noble spirit touched Saul and he swore not to attempt David's life.
Wesley: Saul without doubt meant the oath, feeling a real present change; Clarke: he must have countermanded the previous day's orders.
Henry: Saul's habit of profane swearing makes the oath's sincerity questionable; the conviction was real but soon wore off.

debates.question: Was Saul's oath 'he shall not be slain' sincere?

sides.position: a genuine, real change felt for the present, inclined by God
questionable — Saul swore rashly and his conviction soon wore off (some even suspect a trap)

schools.summary: Gill: Jonathan recalled David from his hiding place and reported all, and Clarke notes David resumed his palace post.
K&D and Ellicott: the reconciliation did not last; the phrase 'as in times past' masks jealousy that a new victory would soon rekindle.
Henry: Jonathan hoped the storm was over and he might keep his father in this good mind — a good hope soon disappointed.
Gill: the renewed war was fought by detachments and skirmishes, not the whole force of either nation.
Henry: David returns good for evil, wielding sword and harp for the king who wronged him; nursed wrath gives place to the devil (Eph. 4:26-27).
Wesley: David continues his good service though ill requited, trusting the bountiful Benefactor of the unthankful.
Gill: David's victories brought back Saul's frantic melancholy, and with it envy, spite, and malice.
Clarke and Wesley: David's success revived jealousy and distraction, exacerbated by diabolic influence as the devil watched his chance.
K&D: 'Ruach Jehovah' marks a judicial hardening God gave Saul up to; Ellicott: a hard saying — evil spirits exist and are permitted to plague as trial or punishment.

debates.question: In what sense is the 'evil spirit' from the LORD?

sides.position: a judicial hardening — God gave impenitent Saul up, the madness inflicted by Jehovah as his higher cause
a fallen spirit permitted by God to punish Saul's desertion, an admittedly unfathomable saying
returning melancholy and envy exacerbated by the devil's watching influence

schools.summary: Gill: Saul aimed to pin David to the wall with the javelin, as he had tried before.
Ellicott identifies the ever-present great spear; K&D: David slipped away and the javelin struck the wall, and he fled that night.
Clarke: distrusting Saul, David stayed on watch, and his bodily agility preserved him.
Henry: rage and the devil drove Saul to murder in his house, but God, undeceived by any plea of madness, preserved David that night.
Gill: Saul, thinking David had gone home to Gibeah, sent men to lie in wait at his house.
Clarke: the morning attack let the killers tell David from Michal and dress the murder as the lawful seizing of a traitor.
Henry: this is the night of Psalm 59; Michal, given as a snare, becomes David's helper — the devil out-shot with his own bow.
Gill: Michal's window-escape echoes Rahab lowering the spies and the disciples lowering Paul at Damascus.
Clarke: with the gate guarded, Michal used a back window where no watchers waited.
Ellicott: this desperate flight began David's long wilderness wanderings; K&D: 'he went, and fled, and escaped.'
Gill: the teraphim were heathen household gods Michal kept secretly; David, ignorant of them, would never have allowed them.
Ellicott: teraphim (like 'penates') is a plural used as singular, a relic of Chaldaean idolatry ranging from Rachel's small image to this life-size figure.
Clarke and Wesley: a human-form image with goats' hair set about its head to counterfeit David's hair in the dim sick-room.

debates.question: What was the 'pillow of goats' hair' at the image's head?

sides.position: goats' hair arranged to mimic David's hair / a muffled sick man's head
a goat's-skin about the head (Syriac, Vulgate), or the LXX's goat's 'liver' to feign breathing (Josephus)

schools.summary: Gill: Saul, suspecting his first messengers negligent or corrupted, sent them (or others) again to take David.
K&D: Michal's 'He is sick,' and later her plea of David's threat, was a prevarication she thought perfectly justifiable.
Henry: Michal's aim to gain time was good, but her lie cannot be justified — God's truth needed not her falsehood.

themes: a right end and a false means

schools.summary: Gill: Saul sent again not to visit David kindly but to verify his person, suspecting deceit since none had actually seen him.
K&D: the command was explicit — 'Bring him up to me in the bed, that I may slay him.'
Henry: to vow death over a supposedly sick man is base and barbarous — unbridled passion grows ever more outrageous.

themes: 'bring him in the bed that I may slay him'

schools.summary: Gill: the messengers came into the room where David was supposed to lie.
K&D: they found only the image and goats'-hair bolster, and the narrative moves on to David's flight to Samuel at Ramah.
Henry: by now David was safe, so Michal was untroubled at the discovery — the delay had served its purpose.
Henry: Saul basely expected Michal to betray her husband; her needless slander of David shows a spirit unlike Jonathan's pious pleading.
Clarke: Michal's words excused her to her father; as a wife sure of David's innocence she could do no less than aid his escape (cf. Ps. 59).
Gill: Saul confronted Michal after the messengers returned and reported the deceit.

debates.question: How should Michal's excuse ('He threatened to kill me') be judged?

sides.position: an unjustified, needless slander of the innocent David, unlike Jonathan's truthful plea
a pardonable self-excuse before her father by a wife sure of her husband's innocence

themes: David and the Son of David wronged by friends

schools.summary: Gill: David fled from his house and escaped the hands of Saul's messengers.
Henry: David ran not to kin or cities but straight to Samuel, making God his refuge for faith, guidance, and the fellowship of praise.
K&D: David came to Samuel both to seek counsel and to be strengthened by the prophet for the trials still ahead.
Gill: officious informers told Saul they had seen David go with Samuel to Naioth in Ramah.
Ellicott: Naioth was not a town but a cluster of dwellings ('house of learning') by Ramah; K&D defend the singular verb and note the rare word for the prophets' 'company.'
Gill: so bent on David's death was Saul that he sent to seize him even in that sacred place.
Clarke and Wesley: the Spirit fell on the messengers so they joined the prophets' exercises (inspired 'as Balaam was') and forgot their errand; Samuel presided to guide and test the gift.
Ellicott: no natural event but the Spirit of God upon them — Ewald pictures the hostile men spell-bound, casting off their garments, drawn into the ecstatic circle.

themes: power over the spirits of men

schools.summary: Gill: Saul learns his messengers, instead of seizing David, are prophesying — and a second and third company fare the same.
Ellicott (with Wordsworth): the church read this passage on Whitsun Tuesday to show the Spirit's free working before the Gospel, paired with Eldad and Medad (Num. 11).

themes: errand of murder turned to praise

schools.summary: Gill: his messengers not returning, Saul finally set out himself from Gibeah to Ramah.
K&D: Saul reached the great well/pit in Sechu near Ramah, asked for Samuel and David, and was directed to Naioth.
Henry: Saul's coming in person shows a persecutor who stoops to anything, baffled by providence yet blind to David's divine protection.
Gill: Saul went on from the well toward Naioth.
K&D: the Spirit of God came upon Saul too, so that he prophesied all the way until he reached Naioth.
Clarke: the Spirit seized him at Sechu, so he prophesied and sang praises onward; Wesley: it fell in the way (not, as on the messengers, at the place) to convince Saul his designs fought against God.

themes: the journey of murder made worship

schools.summary: Gill: Saul stripped off not all but his royal and warlike garments, taking on something of the prophets' habit.

Clarke ties it to Saul's first ecstasy (1 Sam. 10); Wesley: Saul fell senseless in ecstasy a day and night, God chaining him till David escaped and making him 'in some sort a prophet, that he might make David a king.'
Ellicott: the pitiful Spirit pleaded once more with the faithless king, soon forgotten; K&D: 'naked' means only the upper garment laid aside.
Augustine: evil men may possess great gifts — Saul prophesied while persecuting David — but gifts without charity profit nothing (1 Cor. 13).

debates.question: What did the Spirit coming on the faithless Saul signify?

sides.position: a last pitiful pleading of the Spirit with the chosen king, and a restraint to let David escape
a great gift held without charity or obedience, leaving Saul only more accountable
